text: Final Reckoning is a triumphant return
to form for the Mission Impossible
franchise. A superbly written and
brilliantly directed action thriller
with some of the best storytelling I've
ever seen and a glorious sendoff for one
of the most beloved action heroes in
Hollywood history. That is what I wish I
could be telling you right now, but
unfortunately that would be a lie. The
unpleasant truth here is that Final
Reckoning is a real mess of a movie with
a bloated and fragmented storyline that
tries to juggle way too many plot
devices, weighed down by stodgy
exposition dumps that go on forever and
mostly reiterate stuff that we already
know and featuring some of the most
confusing and nonsensical editing
choices in the whole franchise. And it's
such a frustrating disappointment
because most of this film's problems are
self-inflicted and could have been
easily avoided. Now, I know it's just
open, so I'm going to do my best to
avoid spoilers with this one. I'll give
a brief outline of the plot, but I'm not
going to tell you where it's all leading
or how it ends. But if you really don't
want to know anything that happens in
this story, then look away now or
something. You have been warned. So, the
movie opens a few months after the
events of Dead Reckoning. The AI known
as the entity is now wreaking havoc
across the world, spreading
disinformation and stoking conflicts and
civil unrest. Even worse, it's trying to
hack its way into the defense systems of
all the world's major powers to take
control of their nuclear arsenals and
start World War II. Why? Don't know. It
just wants to blow up the world because
it's evil. I guess
really the only chance of stopping it
now lies with Ethan Hunt, who's managed
to obtain the key to the plot device
locked away inside the nuclear sub from
Dead Reckoning. The only problem is that
Gabriel wants the plot device for
himself so that he can take control of
the entity. So, he captures Ethan and
forces him to retrieve it. On the other
hand, the entity wants access to another
plot device where it can lock itself
away and ride out the coming nuclear
apocalypse. And I guess just spend the
rest of eternity chilling out in a
bunker. I don't [ __ ] know. Meanwhile,
Luther is working on another plot device
that will infect the entity with a virus
and stop it from launching the nukes.
But he needs the plot device from the
sub to make it work. So, the plan is for
Ethan to find the sub, retrieve the
sunken plot device, and combine it with
Luther's plot device to poison the
entity and drive it into the other plot
device where they can trap it inside yet
another plot device that Luther also
constructed, but only if they disconnect
it at the exact millisecond that it goes
in.
So, if you're spotting a theme here,
then congratulations, because it
wouldn't be an exaggeration to say that
this script consists of about 80%
contrived plot devices that pop up
whenever they're needed. And that's not
even counting the stuff that's just
blatantly reccon from previous movies.
Like, nobody is believing that the
rabbit's foot from Mission Impossible 3
was a [ __ ] AI. There's just so much
convoluted [ __ ] going on in this
film that it actually becomes hard to
keep track of after a while. And the
thing is, I think the writers realize
this, too, because every 10 minutes or
so, all the major characters gather
around for another big exposition dump
where they basically explain everything
that's going on and everything that each
person has to do to make their plan
work. It's clunky and annoying. It kills
the pace, and it's one of the reasons
why this movie is close to 3 hours long.
And damn, man, you definitely feel it
after a while. The entity itself is a
weak as [ __ ] idea for a villain. Its
motivations and ultimate plan are poorly
defined. There's no explanation for what
it intends to do if it actually wins.
And there's like one scene where it
communicates directly with Ethan, but
then it just becomes this vague threat
in the background that's supposedly
super intelligent, but somehow everyone
can predict what it's going to
do. And I can't shake the feeling that
it was originally going to have a much
more of a presence in the movie, but
they rewrote the script after Dead
Reckoning flopped. Instead, Gabriel
becomes the main antagonist. And well,
there's just not much I can say about
the guy. He's a generic mustache
twirling super villain who wants to take
over the world. The kind of guy you've
seen a million times before. He's
somehow able to throw around 30 megaton
nuclear weapons like they're the kind of
thing you can pick up at a hunting
store, but he also thinks that he can
get clear of the blast within like 15
minutes. Yeah, good luck with that one,
buddy. The editing is also [ __ ]
baffling. The first 15 minutes are this
weird non-chronological mess of
exposition and like three different
infiltration missions happening at the
same time that feels like they tried to
speedrun a much longer segment by just
jamming it all together. It's weird and
annoying and it makes you question when
the actual story line is going to kick
in. There's a big cast of characters in
Ethan's team now, but the script doesn't
seem to know what to do with most of
them. Benji gets sidelined for a lot of
the film and Paris is just kind of
there. like she's got a major beef with
Gabriel because he shoved a knife into
her gut and I assumed it was leading
somewhere except that it doesn't. I
mean, on the plus side, Haley Atwell is
always a pleasure to see on screen, so
that's something at least. If I could
say anything positive about the movie,
the action scenes and the stunts are
still pretty impressive, and they
clearly threw everything they had at
this one. There's a pretty inventive
setpiece inside the submarine as it
rolls around on the seafloor, and the
final fight aboard a plane was visually
cool, even if it doesn't make much sense
in the story. Tom Cruz is wearing well
at 60 and he knows it, which is why he's
shirtless for like half the film. But
the thing is, it feels almost out of
place now. Like maybe this would have
been a good time to acknowledge that
he's getting older and slowing down a
little. But nope, he's just doing his
thing like it's still 2002. Not really
sure why the plot tries to build up
Ethan as some kind of quasi messiah
figure who's destined to save humanity
either, whether it was Tom Cruz's ego or
just a misjudged script. But either way,
it feels weird and self arandizing.
Maybe the biggest issue for me though is
that there's no real sense of closure or
finality to this one despite the name.
It's been built up as the final movie in
the franchise, but there's really no
definitive ending to it. It's really
just treated like business as usual.
Just another adventure, and there's
nothing to suggest they won't roll out
another movie in a few years. But if
Final Reckoning is anything to go by, I
don't know, man. Maybe they should quit
while they're
aheads. Anyway, that's all I've got for
today. This review will self-destruct
after 10
seconds. Go away now.
